text: Sarai had borne Abram no children; she had an Egyptian servant named Hagar.
Sarai told Abram the LORD had kept her from bearing children, so he should sleep with her servant to build a family through her; Abram agreed.
After Abram had lived ten years in Canaan, Sarai gave Hagar to him as a wife.
He slept with Hagar, and she conceived; once she knew she was pregnant, she began to look down on Sarai.
Sarai told Abram the wrong done to her was his fault, and asked the LORD to judge between them.
Abram told Sarai the servant was in her power to treat as she saw fit; Sarai then treated Hagar so harshly she fled.
The angel of the LORD found Hagar by a spring in the wilderness, on the road to Shur.
The angel asked where she had come from and where she was going; she said she was fleeing from her mistress Sarai.
The angel told her to return to her mistress and submit to her.
The angel promised to multiply her offspring so greatly they could not be counted.
The angel said she was pregnant and would bear a son named Ishmael, because the LORD had heard her affliction.
He would be a wild donkey of a man, his hand against everyone and everyone's hand against him, living at odds with all his brothers.
Hagar called the name of the LORD who spoke to her 'You are a God of seeing,' for she had seen the One who saw her.
That is why the well was called Beer-lahai-roi, between Kadesh and Bered.
Hagar bore Abram a son, and Abram named him Ishmael.
Abram was eighty-six years old when Hagar bore Ishmael.
